prompt:
Ticket for the metrics-agent team. Our agent keeps its symbol table in a pickled dict and rewrites the whole thing on every change — 40MB rewrites for one key. I want a real page-oriented B-tree store, pure stdlib, that we own. Deliverable is a package of modules with pagedb.py as the import point: `from pagedb import PageStore, CorruptError`.

Storage model, fixed because ops tooling will poke at these files: one file, fixed-size pages. Page 0 is the header — magic bytes, the page size, committed page count, root page, free-list head, live entry count, and a checksum over the header fields. `PageStore.open(path, page_size=4096)` creates the file if missing; page_size is recorded at creation and the stored value wins on reopen (the argument is only for creation). Open must (a) raise CorruptError if the header checksum doesn't verify or the file is shorter than the committed page count says, and (b) truncate the file back to the committed length if it's longer — a writer that died mid-extend leaves trailing junk we just drop.

API: put(key, value) with str keys (UTF-8, 1..page_size//8 bytes) and bytes values (up to page_size//4 bytes) — ValueError outside those; zero-length values are legal and distinct from missing. get(key) -> bytes or None. delete(key) -> bool (did it exist). scan(lo=None, hi=None) yields (key, value) in key order, lo inclusive, hi exclusive, None means unbounded. len(store) = live entries. flush() is the durability commit point: dirty data pages go out first, header last, and the file is always a whole number of pages afterwards. close() flushes; it's idempotent; any operation on a closed store raises ValueError.

It has to be an actual B-tree: nodes split when their serialized form outgrows the page, deletes borrow/merge on underflow and collapse the root, and pages freed by merges go on a persistent free list threaded through the file. Allocation MUST pop the free list before growing the file — our disks are small and the churn test in the acceptance suite will catch a store that only ever appends. No background compaction, keep it simple.

stats() returns a dict with exactly these keys: page_size, pages (total pages in file), height, nodes, entries, free_pages.

Split the implementation sensibly — I'd expect the page/file layer with the free list, the node serialization, the tree logic, and the facade to be separate modules, not one 600-line file. test_pagedb.py is the acceptance contract: run `python3 test_pagedb.py`, leave the test file alone.
